--- SOURCE 1: In vitro Models of Neurodegenerative Diseases - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7247860/

SUMMARY:
Traditional in vitro cell culture techniques usually involve 2D monolayers in standard tissue-culture plates, Petri dishes or cover slips.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Advances in current in vitro models on neurodegenerative diseases ---
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full

SUMMARY:
Hence, we present a critical review of the latest models used to study neurodegenerative disease, how these models have evolved introducing microfluidics platforms, 3D cell cultures, and the use of induced pluripotent cells to better mimic the neural tissue environment in pathological conditions. In this critical review we want to cover the main features involved when developing an in vitro model for neurodegenerative diseases, aiming attention to the models that use induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC) and microfluidic as the most versatile engineering process to mimic human tissue (Garcia-Leon et al., 2019). Animal cells are the source most used for neurodegenerative disease models and drug development due to their availability and extended knowledge of their phenotypes, but they do not fully predict the complexity of human cells neither in healthy or diseased tissues (Oddo et al., 2019).

Published Time: 2023-11-06

REVIEW article

Front. Bioeng. Biotechnol., 06 November 2023

Sec. Biomaterials

Volume 11 - 2023 | [https://doi.org/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397](https://doi.org/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397)

This article is part of the Research Topic Organ Mimicking Technologies and Their Applications in Drug Discovery[View all 7 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/36668/organ-mimicking-technologies-and-their-applications-in-drug-discovery/magazine)

Advances in current _in vitro_ models on neurodegenerative diseases
===================================================================

[![Image 5: Inês Pereira](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/2129442/74)Inês Pereira](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/2129442)1[![Image 6: Maria J. Lopez-Martinez,,](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1778122/74)Maria J. Lopez-Martinez](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1778122)1,2,3[![Image 7: Josep Samitier,, ](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/158845/74)Josep Samitier](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/158845)1,2,3*

*   1 Nanobioengineering Group, Institute for Bioengineering of Catalonia, Barcelona Institute of Science and Technology, Barcelona, Spain
*   2 Centro Investigación Biomédica en Red: Bioengineering, Biomaterials and Nanomedicine (CIBER-BBN), Instituto de Salud Carlos III (ISCIII), Madrid, Spain
*   3 Department of Electronics and Biomedical Engineering, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain

Many neurodegenerative diseases are identified but their causes and cure are far from being well-known. The problem resides in the complexity of the neural tissue and its location which hinders its easy evaluation. Although necessary in the drug discovery process, _in vivo_ animal models need to be reduced and show relevant differences with the human tissues that guide scientists to inquire about other possible options which lead to _in vitro_ models being explored. From organoids to organ-on-a-chips, 3D models are considered the cutting-edge technology in cell culture. Cell choice is a big parameter to take into consideration when planning an _in vitro_ model and cells capable of mimicking both healthy and diseased tissue, such as induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC), are recognized as good candidates. Hence, we present a critical review of the latest models used to study neurodegenerative disease, how these models have evolved introducing microfluidics platforms, 3D cell cultures, and the use of induced pluripotent cells to better mimic the neural tissue environment in pathological conditions.

Introduction
------------

Neurodegenerative diseases are defined as the progressive loss of neuronal cells and the synapses formed between them, mainly due to the accumulation of proteins such as amyloid beta, tau, or alpha-synuclein ([Kovacs, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B98)). One percent of global mortality is ascribed to neurodegenerative diseases. Projections indicate an upward trend in these numbers with each passing year, primarily due to the extended lifespan of the global population. ([Badhe et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B8)). The number of people with dementia in 2018 was 50 million., two-thirds of these patients go for Alzheimer’s while Parkinson’s disease, epilepsy, Huntington’s, and motor neuron diseases like amyotrophic lateral disease (ALS) are the other one-third ([Patterson, 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B142)). As neurodegenerative diseases do not provoke immediate death, patients’ care treatment tends to last a long period, in which their quality of life decreases with a great economic and emotional price. Consequently, patients and their families are under a big financial burden mainly because they not only need medication but also special care provided only by health professionals and external institutions, worldwide estimated cost of 1 trillion US dollars ([Patterson, 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B142); [Cimler et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B31)). Nonetheless, the numbers for this disease are predicted to triplicate until 2050 and the costs to double by 2030 due to the aging of the population and the increase in the lifespan. ([Patterson, 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B142)).

Even though the discovery of these diseases goes back 100 years, they are still difficult to diagnose and treat ([Möller and Graeber, 1998](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B119)). This is also reflected in the drug development for their treatment. Drug discovery and validation are lengthy and costly processes, mainly due to the strict control necessary to avoid side effects, reducing the number of efficient trials. The billions of dollars invested in drug development are not translated into a high number of drugs approved and the main reason for this is that many preclinical models are not physiologically relevant, failing frequently in the prediction of drug effectiveness in humans ([Ahadian et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B4)). Animal models have been the gold standard in drug development as they allow for testing of drug effectiveness and selectivity in a living organism. However, these models are expensive, time-consuming, ethically questionable, and most of the time unpredictable as clinical trials fail due to the physiological differences between humans and animals ([Zhuang et al., 2017](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B205)). Ineffective drugs must be uncovered in the earlier stages of the process to reduce these costs and the lives of the animals used during the drug development. More efficient drug development can be accomplished through the use of _in vitro_ models capable of recreating the physiological characteristics necessary to test the efficacy and safety of a new drug ([Breslin and O’Driscoll, 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B19)). Understanding the architecture of the tissue, the different cells involved and how they interact is key when these _in vitro_ models are developed to mimic a biologically healthy/diseased tissue or organ. In this critical review we want to cover the main features involved when developing an _in vitro_ model for neurodegenerative diseases, aiming attention to the models that use induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC) and microfluidic as the most versatile engineering process to mimic human tissue ([Garcia-Leon et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B50)).

Brain components to be considered within the model
--------------------------------------------------

Recreating a reliable _in vitro_ model of the brain is a hard task because it is complex to assemble different types of cells, considering cell-cell interactions and the extracellular matrix (ECM). Neurons, despite being the more popular cells, are not the most abundant in the brain, a position occupied by the glia cells family ([Barres, 2008](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B14)). (Fang et al., 2019)This group of cell types encompasses astrocytes and oligodendrocytes, which play roles in supporting and nourishing neurons (Fang et al., 2019). Additionally, microglia protect the neurons and their synapsis, contributing constantly to homeostasis. Increasing evidence suggests that in the case of neuroinflammation, these cells support neuronal destruction and can be active players in the course of neurodegenerative disease ([Tejera and Heneka, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B182)). Other types of cells found in the brain are the neural stem cells (located mainly in the ventral-hypocavitary zone and the hippocampus) and the endothelial cells. The first can differentiate in any neuronal cell type to replace injured or lost ones and the second ones are the main component of the brain vasculature, being essential for the survival of this organ ([Pamies et al., 2014](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B138)). In the case of the functional units of the Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB), pericytes are also present as they are essential for the maintenance of the barrier functions ([van der Helm et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B187)). The constant communication between these cell types is fundamental for the development and the proper function of the brain but they are not easy to mimic _in vitro_. The ECM is valuable for its specific characteristics, formed by lecticans, proteoglycans with a lectin domain, and a hyaluronic acid-binding domain ([Oohashi et al., 2015](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B134)). The native composition of this matrix is characterized by bioactive cues in components such as laminin that influence neural processes as well as particular mechanical characteristics, such as its stiffness that affect the cells. A particular paragraph about this matrix will follow in the next section.

### Cell types utilized in models of neurodegenerative diseases

Animal cells are the source most used for neurodegenerative disease models and drug development due to their availability and extended knowledge of their phenotypes, but they do not fully predict the complexity of human cells neither in healthy or diseased tissues ([Oddo et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B132)). Primary cells are usually harvested from human or animal tissue (although there are some commercially available options), proving a high functional output and reflecting what happens in physiological conditions ([Stacey, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B172)). However, these cells are difficult to purify, they grow at a slower rate, and they can lose their phenotype in culture ([Potjewyd et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B150)). As an alternative to them, Immortalized cell lines, defined as primary cells modified to proliferate indefinitely, are widely used for their ability to grow fast, for maintaining their robustness through a high number of passages, and for their low cost in acquisition and maintenance. Even though these types of cells are very useful, the obtained results can be misleading as they do not render a fair replica of all the cells present in the organ, specifically for transporters, tight junctions, and barrier functions which sometimes can present different values depending on the cell line ([Stacey, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B172); [Stacey, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B172); [Stacey, 2006](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B172); [Rahman et al., 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B154); [Potjewyd et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B150); [Potjewyd et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B150); [Potjewyd et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B150))Besides immortalized cell lines and primary cultures, we can find stem cell cultures but their use is often coupled with ethical problems ([Bordoni et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengineering-and-biotechnology/articles/10.3389/fbioe.2023.1260397/full#B17)). Adult stem cell extraction is difficult or even not feasible ([Zimmermann and Schaffer, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/bioengin

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Cell models for Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease: At the interface of biology and drug discovery
===================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Sandra Cetin, Damijan Knez, Stanislav Gobec, Janko Kos, Anja Pišlar

Show more

Highlights
----------

*   •Treatments that effectively slow the progression of neurodegeneration are lacking. 
*   •Cell-based models of neurodegeneration are pivotal in drug discovery projects. 
*   •Phenotypic screening is useful approach to screen for novel therapeutic molecules. 
*   •Functional assays of neuronal death can be used to screen therapeutic compounds. 

Abstract
--------

[Neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/degenerative-disease "Learn more about Neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are severely debilitating conditions characterized primarily by progressive neuronal loss and impairment of the [nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/nervous-system "Learn more about nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases are the most common [neurodegenerative disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurodegenerative-disorder "Learn more about neurodegenerative disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and their impact is increasing as average life expectancy increases worldwide. Although the underlying mechanisms of both progressive diseases have been extensively studied, we still lack a comprehensive understanding of the molecular basis of both diseases. Current therapeutic options do not slow the progression of the diseases and only provide symptom relief. Cell models that resemble the characteristics of the disease in question are important in [drug discovery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/drug-discovery "Learn more about drug discovery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") projects because they provide information about the therapeutic benefits of drugs under development. Here, we review current _in vitro_ cell models used to study the molecular basis of Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease focusing on their potential for discovering of disease-modifying therapeutics to combat neurodegenerative diseases. We discuss [phenotypic screening](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/phenotypic-screening "Learn more about phenotypic screening from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as an important approach for identifying novel therapeutic molecules. Advances in the development of cell-based assays for drug discovery are discussed, ranging from simple monoculture cell models to high-throughput three-dimensional cell models. Finally, we critically present the limitations of cell models and the caveats encountered in drug discovery to find effective treatment for neurodegenerative diseases.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Neurodegenerative disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurodegenerative-disorder "Learn more about Neurodegenerative disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyotrophic-lateral-sclerosis "Learn more about amyotrophic lateral sclerosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [multiple sclerosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/multiple-sclerosis "Learn more about multiple sclerosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Huntington’s disease, and many others associated with brain pathology currently affect tens of millions of people worldwide [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib2). Unfortunately, as the world's population ages, the incidence of many of these diseases will continue to rise and expectedly more than double by 2050 [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib3). Given the increasing burden and scarcity of effective treatments for these disorders, there is a high demand for new therapies. The key step in the development of therapeutics for [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the establishment of reliable assays for studying [neurodegenerative processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurodegenerative-process "Learn more about neurodegenerative processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and identifying the key culprits of [neurodegeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurodegeneration "Learn more about neurodegeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib4). This review highlights the advances in the development of cell-based assays for [drug development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/drug-development "Learn more about drug development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that facilitate the discovery of therapeutic strategies for [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

2. Causes and consequences of neurodegeneration
-----------------------------------------------

Neurodegeneration is an umbrella term used to describe various [pathological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pathological-process "Learn more about pathological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") occurring in the [central nervous system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/central-nervous-system "Learn more about central nervous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CNS) that result in progressive structural and functional deficits and ultimately the death of neuronal cells [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib5). Many [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") share similar etiologies at the cellular and molecular levels [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib6), yet present distinct characteristics at the phenotypic level, e.g., progressive loss of sensory-motor and cognitive functions [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib8). There are several risk factors associated with AD and PD, including aging, [genetic factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/heredity "Learn more about genetic factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [environmental factors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/environmental-factor "Learn more about environmental factors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as metals, pesticides or [traumatic brain injuries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurotrauma "Learn more about traumatic brain injuries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib9). Nevertheless, a common feature of great significance is the pathological nonreversible association of disease-specific proteins into insoluble aggregates [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib6), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib11). In several [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/degenerative-disease "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), specific proteins begin to aggregate in distinct brain regions in the early, and commonly non-symptomatic, stage of the disease, while additional brain regions become involved in the advanced stages [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib12). AD and PD are the most common [neurodegenerative disorders](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurodegenerative-disorder "Learn more about neurodegenerative disorders from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib6), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib13), exhibiting distinct clinical symptoms and phenotypes ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#tbl0005)) yet sharing common features such as endogenous protein aggregation into misfolded, insoluble inclusions. Namely, aggregated [amyloid β](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/beta-amyloid "Learn more about amyloid β from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Aβ) in [senile plaques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/senile-plaque "Learn more about senile plaques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and hyperphosphorylated [tau protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/tau-protein "Learn more about tau protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in [neurofibrillary tangles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/neurofibrillary-tangle "Learn more about neurofibrillary tangles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are hallmarks of AD pathology [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib16), and α-synuclein (α-syn) in [Lewy bodies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/lewy-body "Learn more about Lewy bodies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Lewy [neurites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurite "Learn more about neurites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are hallmarks of PD pathology ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#fig0005)) [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib18). A plethora of proteins associated with individual [neurodegenerative diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurodegenerative-disorder "Learn more about neurodegenerative diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is known; however, there are still no effective drug candidates to counteract their deleterious roles in the development and progression of neurodegenerative diseases. To this end, determining the causes and consequences of disease-specific [neurodegeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neurodegeneration "Learn more about neurodegeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a prerequisite for the development of effective therapies. Currently, cell assays that resemble different diseases are used as a key step in [drug discovery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/drug-discovery "Learn more about drug discovery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Table 1. The etiology and characteristics of Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease.

| Parameter | Alzheimer’s disease | Parkinson’s disease | References |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Clinical symptoms** | Dementia, depression, psychosis, difficulties speaking, swallowing, and walking | Motor symptoms: bradykinesia, rigidity, rest tremor, postural instability | [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib13), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib20), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib33)] |
| Non-motor symptoms: dementia, psychosis, insomnia, depression, olfactory deficits |
| **Histological hallmarks** | Extracellular senile plaques consisting of amyloid β peptides; intracellular neurofibrillary tangles consisting of hyperphosphorylated tau protein | Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites consisiting of aggregated α-synuclein | [[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib6), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib15), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib17)] |
| **Brain areas affected** | Amygdala, neocortex, hippocampus | _Substantia nigra pars compacta_ | [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib21), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib22), [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib23) |
| **Neural phenotype affected** | Loss of cholinergic neurons | Loss of dopaminergic neurons |  |
| **Cell models** | Toxic models (SH-SY5Y, primary cultures of cortical neurons), genetic models (overexpression of amyloid precursor protein), induced pluripotent stem cells | Toxic models (SH-SY5Y, PC12, MES, primary cultures of cortical neurons), induced pluripotent stem cells | [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib24) |
| **Toxins used in disease modeling** | Amyloid β oligomers and fibrils | 6-hydroxydopamine , 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine , rotenone | [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib24) |

![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0753332222003134-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (230KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0753332222003134-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (230KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0753332222003134-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Schematic presentation of protein aggregation in late-onset neurodegenerative diseases. In Alzheimer ‘s disease, pathology hallmarks are aggregated amyloid β in [senile plaques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/senile-plaque "Learn more about senile plaques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and hyperphosphorylated tau protein in neurofibrillary tangles, whereas α-synuclein in [Lewy bodies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/lewy-body "Learn more about Lewy bodies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Lewy neurites are hallmarks [of Parkinson's disease pathology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pathology-of-parkinsons-disease "Learn more about of Parkinson's disease pathology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Pathological aggregation progressively damages neuronal structure and function.

### 2.1. Alzheimer’s disease

AD is histologically characterized by extracellular [senile plaques](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/senile-plaque "Learn more about senile plaques from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") composed of [Aβ peptides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyloid-beta-protein "Learn more about Aβ peptides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib25) resulting from sequential cleavage of [amyloid precursor protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/amyloid-precursor-protein "Learn more about amyloid precursor protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by [secretases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/secretase "Learn more about secretases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib26). The most common [Aβ peptides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/amyloid-beta-protein "Learn more about Aβ peptides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") consist of 40–42 amino acid residues, and the peptide containing 42 amino acid residues (Aβ 1–42) exhibits increased neurotoxic potential due to its greater susceptibility to aggregation [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib28). The presence of [Aβ peptides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/amyloid-beta-protein "Learn more about Aβ peptides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") triggers a pathogenic cascade leading to the formation of second histological feature of AD - [neurofibrillary tangles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurofibrillary-tangle "Learn more about neurofibrillary tangles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib29), consisting of hyperphosphorylated [tau protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tau-protein "Learn more about tau protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Both Aβ and [tau protein](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/tau-protein "Learn more about tau protein from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") play physiological roles in neurons, namely, regulating synaptic function [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib30) and stabilizing microtubules [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib31), respectively. However, their pathological aggregation progressively damages neuronal structure and function. [Neuronal death](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/neuronal-death "Learn more about Neuronal death from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in AD is mainly observed in the [amygdala](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/amygdala "Learn more about amygdala from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), neocortex, and [hippocampus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/hippocampus "Learn more about hippocampus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib6), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib32). The profound damage and loss of [cholinergic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/cholinergic-receptor-stimulating-agent "Learn more about cholinergic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") neurons in the CNS result in a severe decline in cognitive functions, a major [clinical feature](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/clinical-feature "Learn more about clinical feature from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of AD [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib33). Several hypotheses have been proposed for the development of AD. The most widely accepted hypothesis is the amyloid cascade hypothesis [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib34), followed by the cholinergic hypothesis [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib35), the tau hypothesis [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib36), and the recent [neuroinflammation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nervous-system-inflammation "Learn more about neuroinflammation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") hypothesis [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib37). Each of these hypotheses overlap and complement each other, at least in part, in explaining the complex etiology of AD [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib27), and should therefore be considered when developing cell-based assays for monitoring [neurodegeneration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nerve-degeneration "Learn more about neurodegeneration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#fig0005)).

### 2.2. Parkinson’s disease

The histological hallmarks of PD are round protein inclusions in the neuronal soma, termed [Lewy bodies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/lewy-body "Learn more about Lewy bodies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and filamentous inclusions in neuronal processes, termed Lewy [neurites](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurite "Learn more about neurites from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib38). Both terms represent aggregates of α-syn (the major component) [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib39), and several other proteins, including ubiquitin [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib40) and tau protein [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332222003134#bib41). Analogous to Aβ and tau protein, α-syn has an important physiological role in the CNS related to regulating [neurotransmitter](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/neurotransmitter "Learn more about neurotransmitter from ScienceDirect's AI-generated T

enriched_points.expanded_content: Traditional 2D cell cultures are limited; advanced models like 3D cultures and microfluidics better replicate the neural environment, enhancing disease modeling accuracy.
Transgenic models, particularly using induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), create patient-specific models that mimic neurodegenerative diseases, facilitating personalized therapeutic testing.
Integrating neuroimaging and genetic analysis enhances understanding of disease pathology and progression, aiding in the identification of biomarkers for early diagnosis and treatment.
